title: Distributed Key-Value Store — System Design
description: Design a horizontally scalable, highly available key-value store in the Dynamo/Cassandra lineage. The themes are partitioning by consistent hashing, replication, tunable quorum consistency, and how the system heals divergence after failures.

nodes.label: Client / SDK
nodes.description: Application using the store; talks to any node.

nodes.label: Any Node as Coordinator
nodes.description: Coordinator is a role, not a separate tier: whichever replica node receives a request coordinates replicas for it.

nodes.label: Partitioner (Hash Ring)
nodes.description: Maps a key to its preference list of replica nodes via consistent hashing.

nodes.label: Replica Node A
nodes.description: Owns a range of the keyspace; stores and replicates data.

nodes.label: Replica Node B
nodes.description: Next replica on the ring for the key's preference list.

nodes.label: Replica Node C
nodes.description: Third replica on the ring for the key's preference list.

nodes.label: Per-Replica Local Storage
nodes.description: Every replica node has its own on-disk store (e.g. LSM tree) for the key ranges it owns; shown once but present on each node.

nodes.label: Membership / Gossip
nodes.description: Tracks which nodes are alive and the ring topology via gossip.

nodes.label: Anti-Entropy Service
nodes.description: Background repair of divergent replicas via Merkle-tree comparison.

nodes.label: Hinted-Handoff Store
nodes.description: Temporarily holds writes destined for a down replica, replayed on recovery.

nodes.label: Observability & Admin
nodes.description: Metrics, alerts, admin/control APIs, and backup/restore jobs for operating the ring.

nodes.label: Backup / Snapshot Store
nodes.description: Object storage holding per-token-range snapshots and incremental SSTable backups for restore.

functional: put(key, value) and get(key) over an opaque byte value.
The store scales horizontally: adding nodes increases capacity and throughput.
Survive node and whole-zone failures without losing accepted writes.
nonFunctional: Always available for writes — prefer accepting a write over rejecting it (AP leaning).
Tunable consistency: callers choose how many replicas must respond per operation.
Low, predictable latency (single-digit ms) at the storage layer.
No single point of failure; no special master node.
Incremental scalability — adding/removing a node moves minimal data.

capacity.label: Keys
capacity.note: Working assumption; far beyond one machine.

capacity.label: Value size
capacity.note: Opaque bytes; cap large values (e.g. 1 MB) to bound fanout.

capacity.label: Raw → replicated data
capacity.note: 10B x 1KB = 10TB raw, x N=3 = 30TB stored cluster-wide.

capacity.label: Per-node replica work (steady state)
capacity.note: Reads: 500k x R=2 / 30 ≈ 33k replica reads/s. Writes: 100k x N=3 / 30 ≈ 10k replica writes/s. These are storage-engine ops, not coordinator requests.

capacity.label: Per-node budget (with headroom)
capacity.note: Budget ~1.5x steady state for vnode-ownership imbalance, compaction/repair, and coordinator overhead. Hot-key skew is a separate risk: vnodes smooth range ownership, not one key's traffic — see operations admission control.

capacity.label: Replication factor (N)
capacity.note: Each key on 3 nodes in 3 distinct zones.

capacity.label: Repair / hint budget
capacity.note: Anti-entropy + hint replay throttled so background work can't starve foreground.

capacity.label: Op latency target
capacity.note: Local storage engine + parallel replica calls.

api.description: Write a value into a keyspace. Consistency is expressed with explicit r/w numeric parameters as the canonical convention; symbolic levels (consistency=ONE|QUORUM|ALL) are sugar that maps to r/w per keyspace (e.g. QUORUM → ceil((N+1)/2)). If a request supplies both, the explicit numeric parameter wins and a mismatching symbolic level is rejected as a 400. The partition key is hash(keyspace, key), so keyspace scopes routing, quotas, tombstone identity, backup boundaries, and audit. The coordinator replicates to all N nodes, returns once w ack, and keeps writing to the rest best-effort. timeout_ms bounds how long the coordinator waits for acks before returning 503. Pass the context from a prior GET so the write supersedes that version; a blind PUT with no context becomes a concurrent sibling.

Read a value from a keyspace. The caller picks r (numeric is canonical; consistency=ONE|QUORUM|ALL maps to r per keyspace, with the same precedence rule as PUT — numeric wins, conflicting symbolic level is a 400). The coordinator queries the replicas, returns once r respond or timeout_ms elapses (then 503), and triggers read repair on any stale replicas it saw. If the responses are concurrent it returns every version as a sibling, each with its own context; the caller resolves and PUTs the merged value back. Vector clocks are scoped per (keyspace, key).

Delete a key. This is a tombstone write, not a metadata-free command, so it takes the same consistency vocabulary as PUT: explicit w (numeric canonical; consistency=ONE|QUORUM|ALL maps per keyspace, numeric wins on conflict), the same timeout_ms ack deadline, and the same hash(keyspace, key) routing. It carries and advances the vector clock just like a PUT, replicates to N nodes, waits for w acks, and propagates/repairs like any value. The tombstone is garbage-collected only after a grace period (GC_GRACE) so a stale replica can't resurrect the key during later repair.

dataModel.name: key-value record (per node)
dataModel.note: What each replica stores locally for a key it owns. The partition key is hash(keyspace_id, key), so a key is only meaningful within its keyspace; vector clocks, tombstones, quotas, and backups are all scoped per (keyspace_id, key).

fields.name: keyspace_id
fields.type: string (tenant/keyspace; part of the partition hash and authz scope)

fields.name: key
fields.type: bytes (unique within keyspace; PK is (keyspace_id, key))

fields.name: vector_clock
fields.type: map<nodeId,counter> (causality, pruned to a sibling cap)

fields.name: updated_at
fields.type: timestamp (tiebreak / LWW option / tombstone GC clock)

dataModel.name: ring / token map
dataModel.note: Control-plane state: which token ranges each node owns.

fields.name: node_id
fields.type: owner of the range ending at token

fields.name: zone
fields.type: string (rack/zone, used to place replicas across failure domains)

fields.name: zone
fields.type: string (failure domain — drives zone-aware replica placement)

patterns.name: Consistent hashing
patterns.what: Map keys to nodes on a hash ring so adding/removing a node moves only a fraction of keys.
patterns.whenToUse: Any horizontally-partitioned store that must scale incrementally.
patterns.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

patterns.name: Virtual nodes
patterns.what: Give each physical node many ring positions to even out load and speed rebalancing.
patterns.whenToUse: When physical nodes are few or heterogeneous and naive hashing causes hotspots.
patterns.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

patterns.name: Quorum consistency (N/W/R)
patterns.what: Require W write acks and R read responses; W+R>N gives strong-ish reads.
patterns.whenToUse: When you want a per-operation knob between availability and consistency.
patterns.group: Replicate Each Key to N Nodes

patterns.name: Vector clocks
patterns.what: Per-key causal version vectors to detect concurrent (conflicting) writes.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-writer systems where last-write-wins would silently drop data.

patterns.name: Hinted handoff
patterns.what: Store a write meant for a down replica elsewhere and replay it on recovery.
patterns.whenToUse: To keep accepting writes (preserve W) during transient node failure.

patterns.name: Merkle-tree anti-entropy
patterns.what: Compare replica data via hash trees and repair only the differing ranges.
patterns.whenToUse: Background reconciliation of replicas that diverged during failures.

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Single-Node Key-Value Store (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works: one node running a local storage engine. A client sends get/put to that node, which reads and writes the single on-disk store. For a small dataset this is correct, fast, and trivial to reason about.

It fails on two of the stated requirements at once. Capacity: one machine's disk and memory cap how many keys you can hold, so billions of keys don't fit. Availability: that node is a single point of failure — if it dies or loses its disk, every key is unavailable and accepted writes are gone.

So the baseline motivates the whole design. To hold more than one machine's worth of data you must spread keys across nodes (partitioning); to survive a node loss you must keep more than one copy (replication). Everything that follows removes a specific pain from here.

view.caption: The client sends get/put to a single coordinator node, which reads and writes the one local storage engine.

steps.decisionPrompt: One node holds every key on its local disk. What two requirements break first as data and traffic grow?
whyNow: Establishing the single-node baseline makes it obvious why we partition (capacity) and replicate (availability) — the next two steps each fix one failure of this design.

recap.after: Keys stored and served from one node's local engine.
recap.newRisk: One machine caps capacity, and a single node loss takes all data — must partition and replicate.

traps.trap: Shipping a single beefy node ('just use a bigger box') as the answer.
traps.why: It caps at one machine's disk/RAM and is a single point of failure — a node loss loses every accepted write.
traps.instead: Partition keys across many nodes for capacity and replicate each key for survival.

steps.title: 2. Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing
description: Place nodes on a hash ring; a key hashes to a position and is owned by the next node clockwise. Adding or removing a node only reshuffles the keys between it and its neighbor, not the whole keyspace — that is what makes scaling incremental.
Give each physical node many virtual nodes (tokens) so load is even and a departing node's range is absorbed by many peers rather than one.
Any node can receive a request and act as coordinator; it uses the ring to find the key's owners.

Hashing deliberately scatters keys, so ordered range scans (e.g. 'all keys a..c') are out of scope — that's the price of even distribution, and fine for an opaque-value KV store. Note that virtual nodes spread ranges, not a single hot key: one very hot key still lands on one preference list and needs its own mitigation (a front cache, client coalescing, or coordinator-side hot-key rate limiting — see the operations step's admission control).

view.caption: The client reaches any coordinator, which consults the hash ring to find the key's owner among replica nodes A, B, and C.

options.name: Consistent hashing + virtual nodes (default)
options.description: Each physical node claims many token positions on a hash ring, so a key hashes to a vnode and is owned by the next node clockwise.
pros: Adding or removing a node moves only ~1/N of keys, not the whole keyspace.
Virtual nodes spread each physical node's load and let many peers absorb a departing node's range.
No master: any node can hash a key to its owners from its ring view.
cons: No key ordering, so range scans (e.g. 'all keys a..c') are not supported.
Ring view is eventually consistent, so a stale view costs an extra hop.
Hot single keys still land on one owner — vnodes spread ranges, not individual keys.

view.caption: The coordinator hashes the key on the ring to pick its owner among replicas A, B, and C.

options.name: Range (order-preserving) partitioning
options.description: Keys are split into contiguous sorted ranges, each assigned to a node via a range map, so adjacent keys live together.
pros: Keys stay sorted, so range scans and ordered iteration are cheap.
A split/merge of ranges is conceptually simple and explicit.
Maps naturally onto sorted on-disk structures (e.g. LSM/SSTables).
cons: Sequential keys (timestamps, monotonic IDs) create hotspots on one range owner.
Rebalancing requires actively splitting/moving ranges — often via a coordinator.
Uneven key distribution needs ongoing range re-splitting to stay balanced.

view.caption: The coordinator looks up a range map (a-h, h-p, p-z) to route the key to the node owning that range.

options.name: Modulo hashing (key % numNodes)
options.description: A key's hash is taken modulo the node count to pick its owner directly, with no ring or range structure to maintain.
pros: Trivial to implement and reason about for a fixed cluster.
Even key distribution when node count is stable.
No ring or range-map structure to maintain.
cons: Changing the node count remaps almost every key — a cluster-wide data storm.
Effectively unusable for incremental scaling, which is a core requirement.
No graceful story for heterogeneous nodes or single-node failure.

view.caption: The coordinator computes hash(key) % N to pick a fixed bucket node, with no ring or range structure.

steps.decisionPrompt: With plain modulo hashing, what fraction of keys move when you add the Nth node? With consistent hashing?

concepts.definition: Hash both keys and nodes onto a ring; a key belongs to the next node clockwise.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adding/removing a node moves ~1/N of keys instead of nearly all of them.
concepts.example: 10 nodes -> add one -> only ~9% of keys relocate.
concepts.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

concepts.definition: Each physical node claims many ring positions.
concepts.whyItMatters: Smooths load imbalance and spreads a failed node's range across many peers.
concepts.example: 256 vnodes per physical node is common.
concepts.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

whyNow: Partitioning is the foundation — every later decision (replication, quorum, repair) is defined relative to a key's position on the ring.

recap.after: Keys spread across many nodes; incremental scaling.
recap.newRisk: A single copy per key — a node loss loses data; need replication next.

traps.why: Changing node count remaps almost every key, causing a storm of data movement.
traps.instead: Hash onto a ring with virtual nodes so only neighbors' ranges shift.

steps.title: 3. Replicate Each Key to N Nodes
description: Store each key on the next N distinct nodes clockwise from its position — its preference list. Build it by walking the ring and taking the next N distinct *physical* nodes, skipping extra vnodes that map back to a node already chosen, and preferring nodes in distinct zones/racks so one zone outage can't take all N copies. N=3 across 3 zones is typical.

The coordinator forwards every write to all N replicas and every read to all N (or enough of them). A replica's ack means it has *durably* recorded the write (committed to its commit log / on-disk store), not merely received it in memory — otherwise a crash right after ack would lose an 'accepted' write.
Replication is what makes the store durable and available: any replica can serve the key.

view.caption: The coordinator uses the ring to locate the preference list and forwards each write to replica nodes A, B, and C, each backed by its local storage.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you choose the N replicas so a single zone outage can't take all copies of a key?
whyNow: Durability and availability both come from replication; it sets up the consistency knob in the next step.

recap.newRisk: Replicas can disagree; need rules for how many must respond.

flows.name: Write replicated to N nodes
flows.note: The coordinator forwards a write to every node on the key's preference list so each holds a copy.

steps.title: 4. Tunable Consistency with Quorums
description: Let callers pick W (write acks required) and R (read responses required). If W + R > N, any read set overlaps at least one node that saw the latest write — read-your-writes without a master.
Common defaults: N=3, W=2, R=2. Want faster writes? Lower W to 1. Want stronger reads? Raise R to 3. The knob is per operation, not a cluster-wide setting.
The coordinator returns success as soon as the quorum is met, not after all N — that's what keeps latency low and availability high.

view.caption: The client's request reaches the coordinator, which contacts replicas A, B, and C and returns as soon as the W or R quorum responds.

options.name: Leaderless tunable quorum (default)
options.description: The coordinator writes to N replicas and returns once W ack; reads gather R responses, so any node can serve any operation.
pros: Per-operation dial: callers pick W and R to trade latency for consistency.
W+R>N gives read-your-writes with no master and no failover step.
Any node coordinates, so there is no single point of failure.
cons: Concurrent writes still produce divergent versions to reconcile.
Does not give full linearizability — only strong-ish overlap reads.
Callers must understand the knob; a wrong (W,R) silently weakens guarantees.

view.caption: The coordinator fans the request to all three replicas and acks once W writes (or R reads) respond — no leader.

options.name: Leader-per-key (single-primary) replication
options.description: Each key has one elected primary that orders all its writes and replicates them to followers, which serve reads.
pros: A single primary orders all writes for a key, giving clean linearizable reads from the leader.
No sibling/conflict reconciliation — the leader is the arbiter.
Familiar primary/replica mental model.
cons: Leader failure needs election/failover before writes resume — a brief unavailability window.
Goes against the AP, always-writable requirement during partitions.
The leader is a per-key write bottleneck and hotspot.

view.caption: The coordinator writes to the key's leader (node A), which replicates to followers B and C.

options.name: Sloppy quorum + hinted handoff (max availability)
options.description: When preferred replicas are down, writes go to any N healthy nodes that hold a hint and later hand the data back on recovery.
pros: Stays writable even when some preference-list nodes are unreachable — write to any N healthy nodes.
Hinted handoff replays to the intended replica on recovery, so data still lands.
Best fit for the 'prefer accepting a write over rejecting it' goal.
cons: W+R>N no longer strictly guarantees overlap, so reads can miss a fresh write.
Requires version reconciliation (vector clocks) to heal afterwards.
Hints can pile up and need replay/cleanup machinery.

view.caption: With a replica unreachable, the coordinator writes to healthy node A plus a hint store standing in for the down replica.

steps.decisionPrompt: With N=3, which (W,R) pairs give strong reads, and which favor write availability?

concepts.definition: Requiring overlapping read and write sets so a read sees the latest committed write.
concepts.whyItMatters: Gives a consistency dial without a single leader.
concepts.example: N=3, W=2, R=2: write set and read set share at least one node.

whyNow: This is the heart of a Dynamo-style store — articulating the W+R>N tradeoff is what the interviewer is listening for.

recap.after: Per-op consistency dial via W and R.
recap.newRisk: Below-quorum or concurrent writes create divergent versions to reconcile.

traps.why: Concurrent writes and sloppy quorums (hinted handoff) can still produce conflicting versions.
traps.instead: Say W+R>N gives strong-ish read-your-writes, and pair it with version reconciliation.

steps.title: 4a. Choosing N, W, and R
description: The quorum knobs are a small decision with outsized effect. With N replicas, requiring W write acks and R read responses, the rule W + R > N guarantees any read set overlaps any write set — so a read sees the latest committed write (read-your-writes) without a leader.

Tune for the workload (N=3): W=1,R=1 is lowest latency and stays writable with 2 nodes down, but reads can be stale (W+R=2, no overlap). W=2,R=2 is the common quorum — read-your-writes, survives one node down on both paths. W=3,R=1 gives durable writes and fast reads but a single down replica blocks writes. W=1,R=3 maximizes write availability but reads must reach all 3, so any down replica blocks reads. These are per-operation dials, not a global setting.

Behavior under one node failure follows directly: a quorum survives a single failure only if the required count still fits in the 2 live replicas — so W=2/R=2 ride through one down node, while W=3 or R=3 do not.

Sloppy quorums (writing to whatever N nodes are reachable, with hinted handoff) trade some consistency for availability during failures — useful, but it means W+R>N no longer strictly guarantees overlap, so reconcile with versioning.

view.caption: The coordinator dispatches to replicas A, B, and C, waiting for W acks (or R reads) per the chosen quorum values.

steps.decisionPrompt: With N=3, which (W,R) pairs give read-your-writes, and which favor write availability?

concepts.definition: Choosing write/read quorum sizes whose sets always overlap, giving strong-ish reads without a leader.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the precise lever between consistency and availability, tunable per operation.
concepts.example: N=3,W=2,R=2 overlaps; W=1,R=1 is fast but may read stale.

traps.trap: Claiming a quorum gives full linearizability.
traps.why: Concurrent writes and sloppy quorums still produce divergent versions to reconcile.
traps.instead: Say W+R>N gives read-your-writes, and pair it with version reconciliation.

recap.after: N/W/R chosen per workload with the W+R>N rule understood.
recap.newRisk: Sloppy quorums and concurrent writes still need version reconciliation (next step).

steps.title: 5. Detect and Reconcile Conflicting Writes
description: When two clients write the same key concurrently (or during a partition), replicas end up holding different versions. The store must not silently drop one.

Attach a vector clock to each value. On read, if versions are causally ordered, return the newest; if they're concurrent, return both as siblings for the application (or a registered merge function) to reconcile.

Resolution is a closed loop: the GET returns the siblings, the application merges them (e.g. union the two cart states), and the client PUTs the merged value back with a context that descends from *both* sibling clocks. That single write now supersedes both versions, and read repair / anti-entropy converge the replicas — siblings don't accumulate forever.

Bound the metadata: cap the number of siblings (drop or coalesce the oldest beyond a limit) and prune vector-clock entries (e.g. keep the most recent N (node, counter) pairs, evict stale ones) so a long-lived hot key can't grow an unbounded clock.

Deletes ride the same machinery as a tombstone: a delete is a write of value=null with deleted=true and an advanced vector clock. It replicates and repairs like any value, so a stale replica that comes back can't silently revive the key. Tombstones are kept for a grace period (GC_GRACE, longer than the worst expected repair lag), then garbage-collected once every replica is known to have seen them.
Last-write-wins (by timestamp) is the simpler alternative, but it can lose writes under clock skew — call out the tradeoff.

view.caption: The coordinator reads the same key from replicas A and B and compares their versions to detect concurrent writes.

options.name: Vector clocks + siblings (default)
options.description: Each value carries a vector clock; on read the store returns the causally newest version, or surfaces concurrent versions as siblings to merge.
pros: Distinguishes 'supersedes' from 'concurrent', so no write is silently dropped.
Concurrent versions surface as siblings for the app or a merge function to reconcile.
Principled answer for a store that accepts writes during partitions.
cons: The application (or a registered merge) must handle siblings — more client complexity.
Vector clocks can grow with the number of writers and need pruning.
Read path may return multiple versions, complicating callers.

view.caption: The coordinator reads replicas A {A:2} and B {A:1,B:1}, sees the versions are concurrent, and returns both as siblings.

options.name: Last-write-wins (timestamp)
options.description: Each value carries a wall-clock timestamp and the replica with the highest timestamp wins; all other versions are discarded.
pros: Dead simple: keep the value with the highest timestamp, discard the rest.
No siblings, no merge logic — reads always return one value.
Cheap to store and reason about.
cons: Clock skew lets a stale write 'win', silently destroying a newer value.
Unacceptable where every write matters (e.g. add-to-cart, balance updates).
Hides conflicts instead of surfacing them.

view.caption: The coordinator compares replica timestamps and keeps node A's newer value (ts 10:02) over node B's.

options.name: Server-side CRDT merge
options.description: Values are conflict-free replicated data types (counters, sets, registers) that the server merges deterministically into one value on read.
pros: Conflict-free data types (counters, sets, registers) merge deterministically on the server.
No siblings leak to the application — reads return a single merged value.
Great fit for collaborative or commutative workloads (e.g. shopping carts).
cons: Only works for values that fit a CRDT; opaque bytes can't be merged this way.
CRDT metadata adds storage and complexity per value.
Constrains the data model — not a general opaque-value store anymore.

view.caption: Replicas A and B feed their states into a deterministic merge so the coordinator returns one converged value.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two clients update a shopping cart concurrently on different replicas. How does the store avoid losing an item?

concepts.definition: A per-key map of nodeId -> counter capturing causal history of a value.
concepts.whyItMatters: Distinguishes 'this version supersedes that' from 'these happened concurrently'.
concepts.example: Cart versions {A:2} and {A:1,B:1} are concurrent -> merge as siblings.

whyNow: A store that accepts writes during partitions must have an answer for divergence; vector clocks are the principled one.

recap.before: Versions assumed to be totally ordered.
recap.after: Concurrent versions detected and surfaced for merge; deletes are tombstones.
recap.newRisk: Application must handle siblings; vector clocks/tombstones need pruning and GC.

flows.name: Sibling merge write-back
flows.note: The read returns concurrent siblings; the client merges them and writes the result back with a context descending from both, so replicas converge to one value.

traps.trap: Defaulting to last-write-wins without mentioning the risk.
traps.why: Clock skew makes a stale write 'win', silently destroying a newer value.
traps.instead: Use vector clocks (or LWW only where loss is acceptable, stated explicitly).

traps.trap: Implementing delete by physically removing the value immediately.
traps.why: A replica that was down during the delete still holds the old value; on later anti-entropy it looks 'newer' than 'absent' and resurrects the deleted key.
traps.instead: Write a tombstone with an advanced vector clock, replicate and repair it like a value, and GC only after a grace period once all replicas have seen it.

steps.title: 6. Tolerate Failures: Hinted Handoff & Anti-Entropy
description: If a replica is temporarily down, the coordinator still needs W acks to accept the write. With hinted handoff it writes to a substitute node, tagging the write with a hint naming the intended replica; when that replica recovers, the hint is replayed to it.

Bound the hints so they can't become a second database: each hint has a TTL (e.g. a few hours) and there's a per-destination quota; replay is rate-limited so a recovering node isn't flooded. If a replica stays down past the TTL (treated as permanently lost), hints are dropped and full anti-entropy / a replacement-node bootstrap take over instead.

Replicas still drift over time (dropped hints, longer outages). A background anti-entropy process keeps a per-range Merkle tree per node, compares trees with peers on a low-priority schedule, and repairs only the key ranges whose hashes differ — cheap reconciliation. Repair runs within a bandwidth budget (a few % of node I/O) and can be operator-triggered on demand after an incident.

Read repair complements this: on a quorum read that discovers some replicas returned a stale (or missing) version, the coordinator writes the latest version back to those replicas inline, so frequently-read keys self-heal without waiting for the next anti-entropy pass.

view.caption: With a replica down, the coordinator writes to the healthy nodes plus a hint store, while the anti-entropy service repairs replicas in the background.

steps.decisionPrompt: A replica is down for an hour. How do you keep accepting writes, and how is it brought back in sync?

concepts.definition: Temporarily storing a write for a down replica on another node, replayed on recovery.
concepts.whyItMatters: Preserves write availability (W) during transient failures.
concepts.example: Node B down -> write goes to D with hint 'for B' -> replayed when B returns.

concepts.definition: Hash-tree comparison of replica data to find and repair only divergent ranges.
concepts.example: Top hashes match -> in sync; differ -> descend to find the divergent range.

whyNow: Failure handling is where availability is actually earned; naming hinted handoff + Merkle anti-entropy shows real distributed-systems depth.

recap.before: A down replica blocks quorum or causes data loss.
recap.after: Writes still succeed; replicas re-converge in the background.
recap.newRisk: Hints can pile up; anti-entropy and read-repair cost background bandwidth.

failureDrills.scenario: A node is down during many writes, then rejoins.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Hinted writes are replayed; remaining drift is closed by anti-entropy/read-repair.
failureDrills.mitigation: Hinted handoff + Merkle-tree repair + read repair together.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Both sides keep accepting writes (AP); on heal, versions reconcile via vector clocks.

flows.name: Hinted handoff while a replica is down
flows.note: A down replica can't ack, so the coordinator stores a hint on a substitute and replays it on recovery — the write still reaches W.

flows.name: Read repair on a stale replica
flows.note: A quorum read finds one replica behind; the coordinator returns the newest version to the client and writes it back to the stale replica inline.

bottlenecks.issue: Hint store fills up when a replica is down for a long time.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-destination hint quota + TTL; past the TTL, drop hints and rely on anti-entropy / replacement-node bootstrap.

bottlenecks.issue: Anti-entropy repair competes with foreground reads/writes.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Low-priority schedule, per-range Merkle trees, and a bandwidth budget (few % of node I/O); operator-triggerable after incidents.
Decommission it from the ring and bootstrap a replacement that streams its ranges from the surviving replicas.

steps.title: 7. Membership, Gossip, and Rebalancing
description: There is no master. Nodes learn who is alive and who owns what via a gossip protocol — each node periodically exchanges state with a few peers, so topology and liveness converge across the cluster on their own.

Joining is a state machine, not a flip: (1) the new node picks its vnode tokens and gossips state=joining; (2) it bootstraps — streaming the key ranges it will own from the current owners, at a throttled rate, while the old owners keep serving; (3) once a range is fully streamed it becomes a pending replica that takes new writes; (4) the node gossips state=up and ownership becomes active; (5) the old owners drop the ranges they no longer own and reclaim space. Leaving runs the same machine in reverse: stream ranges out, then decommission. Virtual nodes keep each transfer small and parallel.

Replica placement is zone-aware: when the ring resolves a key's preference list it skips candidates until it has N nodes in distinct zones/racks (each node gossips its zone), so the N copies survive a whole-zone outage rather than just N random nodes.
Clients/coordinators route using their (eventually consistent) view of the ring; a stale view just means an extra hop, not incorrect data.

view.caption: The coordinator learns the ring and liveness from the membership/gossip layer, which exchanges state with replicas A, B, and C.

options.name: Gossip-based membership (default)
options.description: Each node periodically exchanges liveness and ring state with a few random peers, so topology and failure detection converge with no master.
pros: Fully decentralized: no master to fail, fitting the no-SPOF requirement.
Scales to hundreds of nodes; state converges in O(log N) gossip rounds.
Nodes detect peer failures locally (e.g. phi-accrual) without a central authority.
cons: Topology view is eventually consistent — a stale view costs an extra hop.
Convergence takes several rounds, so membership changes aren't instant.
Debugging emergent gossip behavior (flapping, partitions) is harder.

view.caption: The coordinator reads its ring view from the gossip layer, which spreads liveness among replicas A, B, and C.

options.name: Central coordinator service (ZooKeeper/etcd)
options.description: A strongly-consistent coordinator service holds the authoritative membership and ring, and nodes read topology from it.
pros: A single authoritative, strongly-consistent view of liveness and the ring.
Membership changes are instant and unambiguous — no convergence delay.
Simpler to reason about and debug than emergent gossip.
cons: Reintroduces a critical dependency that must itself be made HA (a quasi-master).
The coordinator can become a scaling and availability bottleneck.
If it's unreachable, the cluster can't safely change topology.

view.caption: The coordinator queries a central config service that tracks each replica's liveness via heartbeats.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why prefer gossip-based membership over a central coordinator service tracking liveness?

concepts.definition: Nodes periodically exchange state with random peers; information spreads epidemically.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decentralized failure detection and topology with no single point of failure.
concepts.example: Each node gossips with 3 peers per second; the cluster converges in O(log N) rounds.

whyNow: Closing on decentralized membership ties the design back to the 'no SPOF / incremental scalability' non-functional requirements.

recap.after: Self-managing cluster that scales and heals without a master.
recap.newRisk: Eventual-consistency of the ring view; flapping nodes; rebalancing bandwidth.

bottlenecks.issue: Rebalancing floods the network when a big node joins.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Throttle streaming; virtual nodes spread the transfer across many peers.
Phi-accrual failure detector with hysteresis before declaring down.

flows.name: A new node joins and bootstraps its ranges
flows.note: Joining is a throttled state machine: the new node gossips joining, streams its ranges from the old owner while that owner keeps serving, then flips to active so reads/writes route to it and the old owner drops the ranges.

messages.label: range streamed: pending replica, takes new writes

steps.title: 8. Operate It: Observability, Security, and Backup/Restore
description: Correctness in a Dynamo-style store is the easy part; keeping latency predictable while background work runs is the real job — so make that work visible and bounded. Export per-consistency-level latency (ONE/QUORUM/ALL), quorum-failure rate, stale-read and read-repair rate, sibling count per key, hinted-handoff backlog and replay rate, anti-entropy bytes repaired, gossip convergence time, and rebalance/bootstrap progress. Alert on the leading indicators — growing hint backlog, rising sibling counts, repair falling behind — before they become user-visible.

Admission control protects foreground traffic and isolates tenants: enforce per-keyspace request and storage quotas, cap value size, rate-limit hot keys at the coordinator, and throttle repair, hint replay, compaction, and bootstrap streaming to a fixed I/O budget so background jobs can never starve gets and puts.

Security: authenticate clients (mutual TLS or tokens), authorize per keyspace/tenant so the keyspace in the request path (hash(keyspace, key)) is also the unit of authorization — keys aren't a single flat namespace. Encrypt in transit (TLS between client↔coordinator and node↔node) and at rest, and audit administrative actions (ring changes, decommission, manual repair) and per-keyspace access since those can move or delete data cluster-wide.

Backup/restore: snapshot each token range to object storage with incremental SSTable backups between snapshots; restore by streaming a range's backup into a replacement node and then running anti-entropy to validate it against the live replicas. Because backups are per range — and ranges are keyed by hash(keyspace, key) — a single node, zone, or keyspace can be restored without touching the rest of the ring.

view.caption: The observability/admin plane scrapes metrics and drives ring admin, while replicas snapshot their token ranges to a backup store for restore.

steps.decisionPrompt: Repair, compaction, and hint replay all run in the background. How do you keep them from blowing your read/write latency SLO?

concepts.definition: Bounding background work (repair, hint replay, compaction, bootstrap) and abusive foreground load (hot keys, large values) to a fixed resource budget.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's what keeps a correct-but-busy store from missing its latency SLO; correctness alone doesn't bound tail latency.
concepts.example: Anti-entropy capped at a few % of node I/O; hot-key requests rate-limited at the coordinator.
concepts.group: Operate It: Observability, Security, and Backup/Restore

whyNow: A book-ready answer doesn't stop at the happy path — production realism (metrics, throttles, security, backup/restore) is what separates a senior answer from a textbook one.

recap.before: Correct core design, but background work and operations implicit.
recap.after: Bounded, observable repair/handoff; per-keyspace auth and encryption; per-range backup/restore.
recap.newRisk: Operational surface (metrics pipeline, backup storage, admin auth) is now part of what must stay available.

bottlenecks.issue: Background repair/compaction/hint replay eats foreground latency.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Throttle each to a fixed I/O budget; schedule repair at low priority and operator-trigger after incidents.

bottlenecks.issue: A hot key or oversized value saturates one preference list.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Coordinator-side rate limiting + value-size cap; front the hottest keys with a cache.

traps.trap: Leaving repair, compaction, and hint replay unthrottled because they're 'just background'.
traps.why: Unbounded background I/O competes with gets/puts and blows tail latency even though the data is correct.
traps.instead: Give every background job an explicit I/O budget and surface its backlog as a first-class metric.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Dynamo-Style KV Store
finalDesign.description: A masterless ring of nodes (with virtual nodes) partitions keys by consistent hashing and replicates each to N nodes across distinct zones. The 'coordinator' is a role, not a tier — any replica node that receives a request coordinates it, and every replica has its own local storage engine. Callers pick W/R per operation for the consistency/availability dial. Concurrent writes are reconciled with vector clocks and siblings (deletes are tombstones); transient failures use bounded hinted handoff, and replicas re-converge via Merkle-tree anti-entropy and inline read repair. Membership, liveness, and zone topology spread by gossip. An observability/admin plane keeps background work throttled and observable, and each token range is snapshotted to a backup store for restore.

view.caption: The client reaches any node acting as coordinator, which partitions by the ring, replicates to per-replica-local-storage nodes A/B/C, and is supported by gossip membership, hinted handoff, anti-entropy + read repair, an observability/admin plane, and per-range backups.

functional.how: Coordinator routes to the preference list; delete is a tombstone write that replicates and GCs after a grace period.
Consistent hashing + virtual nodes move minimal data when nodes are added.
Zone-aware placement puts the N replicas in distinct zones; hinted handoff, anti-entropy, and read repair heal drift.

nonFunctional.how: Sloppy quorum + hinted handoff keep accepting writes during failures.
Per-op W and R with the W+R>N rule.
Masterless ring; gossip membership; any node coordinates.
Virtual nodes spread token ranges so joins/leaves move little data.
Throttled repair/hint replay/compaction within an I/O budget, plus admission control on hot keys and large values; all surfaced as metrics.
Per-keyspace auth + TLS/encryption, audited admin actions, and per-token-range backup/restore validated by anti-entropy.

tradeoff: Self-hosted Cassandra, ScyllaDB, or Riak-style systems expose the ring, quorum, repair, compaction, and capacity knobs this interview teaches.
Managed KV and wide-column products hide most of that machinery behind an API, autoscaling, and provider-run operations, but reduce control over replica placement and repair behavior.
makesIrrelevant: A managed Dynamo-style service removes most custom ownership of the partitioner, hinted-handoff store, anti-entropy scheduler, gossip membership, and rebalancing workflow.

technologyChoices.concern: Partitioning, request routing, and node discovery
tradeoff: Any-node coordination needs clients to find healthy nodes without pinning to a single master; load balancers and service discovery help with health, not key ownership.
The hash ring still belongs inside the KV layer because generic L7 routing cannot route by token range unless you build custom routing logic.
makesIrrelevant: Managed discovery and load balancing remove most hand-maintained seed-node lists, static DNS failover, and manual connection rotation in clients.

technologyChoices.concern: Per-replica storage engine and local disk layout
tradeoff: LSM-style engines fit write-heavy replicated KV workloads because writes are append-friendly and background compaction can be budgeted.
Running the storage engine yourself gives you tombstone, compaction, compression, and snapshot control, but foreground latency now depends on your I/O scheduling discipline.
makesIrrelevant: A managed KV removes direct tuning of SSTables, WALs, compaction, and per-node snapshot plumbing, replacing them with service-level capacity and consistency knobs.

tradeoff: Leaderless quorum stores preserve write availability during many failures and partitions, but callers must understand stale reads, sloppy quorums, and conflicting versions.
CP stores such as Raft-backed systems, Spanner, or FoundationDB simplify the application model with stronger ordering, but writes can block when consensus cannot form.
makesIrrelevant: Choosing a CP store removes most vector-clock, sibling, read-repair, and sloppy-quorum discussion, but no longer satisfies the always-writable AP requirement.

tradeoff: Vector clocks and siblings preserve concurrent writes without pretending the system can pick a universally correct winner.
Last-write-wins is operationally simpler but can silently lose data under clock skew; CRDTs avoid some manual merges but only fit data types with well-defined merge rules.
makesIrrelevant: A strongly consistent store or narrow conditional-write API removes sibling return paths for those keys, but it also changes the availability and latency envelope.

technologyChoices.concern: Hinted handoff, repair, and rebalancing jobs
tradeoff: Repair, hint replay, bootstrap, and decommission workflows need durable progress, retries, and throttling because they compete with foreground get/put latency.
A workflow engine makes these jobs observable and restartable, but it does not replace per-range I/O budgets, idempotent replay, or safety checks around tombstone grace.
makesIrrelevant: Provider-managed repair and lifecycle workflows remove most custom cron, retry-table, and operator-runbook glue for background maintenance.

tradeoff: Pure gossip keeps the ring decentralized and avoids a master node, but convergence, split-brain suspicion, and operator visibility become application concerns.
A CP metadata source is easier to reason about for joins and decommissions, but it can become an availability dependency if every request must consult it.
makesIrrelevant: Using a managed KV or provider-owned node lifecycle removes most custom bootstrap, failure-detection, and token-movement state machines.

tradeoff: Object storage is the natural landing zone for per-token-range snapshots because it is cheap, durable, and decoupled from the live replica fleet.
Snapshots are not enough by themselves; restore drills and anti-entropy validation are what prove the backup can rejoin the ring safely.
makesIrrelevant: A durable managed object store removes most custom backup repository replication, lifecycle tiering, and cross-zone copy work.

technologyChoices.concern: Observability, repair lag, and SLO dashboards
tradeoff: A KV store needs first-class metrics for quorum latency, failed replica calls, repair lag, hint backlog, tombstone age, compaction debt, and token imbalance.
Self-hosted telemetry gives portability and detailed control, while managed telemetry reduces toil but can make high-cardinality per-keyspace metrics expensive.
makesIrrelevant: Managed metrics, logs, traces, and dashboards remove much of the custom scrape, retention, and alert-routing infrastructure around the store.

technologyChoices.concern: Security, keyspace isolation, and secret management
tradeoff: Per-keyspace auth, TLS, encryption keys, and audited admin actions have to sit beside the data path; adding them after the ring works is how multi-tenant KV stores leak boundaries.
Provider KMS, IAM, and audit logs reduce cryptographic and compliance toil, but the application still owns keyspace-level authorization and noisy-neighbor limits.
makesIrrelevant: Managed identity and secrets remove most custom certificate rotation, KMS envelope-key storage, and admin-audit plumbing.

say: Confirm AP leaning: prefer availability/partition tolerance over strict consistency.
Establish it must be masterless and incrementally scalable.
Agree on tunable consistency as a goal, not one fixed level.
Partition with consistent hashing + virtual nodes.
Replicate to N nodes across zones.
Introduce W/R quorums and the W+R>N rule.
Handle concurrent writes with vector clocks and siblings, including the merge write-back loop and tombstone deletes.
Cover failures: bounded hinted handoff (TTL/quota), Merkle anti-entropy, read repair.
Explain gossip membership, the join/bootstrap state machine, and zone-aware placement.
Map each requirement to a mechanism.
State tradeoffs: eventual consistency, sibling handling, repair bandwidth.
Show production controls: throttled background work, metrics, per-keyspace auth, per-range backup/restore.
Note when you'd instead pick a CP store (need linearizability).

expectations: Partitions with consistent hashing and replicates to N nodes.
Reasons about sloppy quorums, partition behavior, and CAP positioning.
Discusses failure detection (phi-accrual), rebalancing cost, and when a CP design is the right call instead.
Bounds the operational surface: throttled repair/hint replay, tombstone GC grace, per-keyspace auth, and per-range backup/restore.

followUps: When would you choose a CP store (e.g. Raft-based) over this AP design?
How do vector clocks grow unbounded, and how do you prune them safely?
How would you add range scans or secondary indexes to a hash-partitioned store?
How does a sloppy quorum differ from a strict quorum, and what does it cost in consistency?
How would you back up and restore a single token range without downtime?
How do you GC tombstones safely, and what goes wrong if the grace period is shorter than your worst repair lag?
Which background jobs (repair, compaction, hint replay, bootstrap) compete for I/O, and how do you budget them so foreground latency stays within SLO?

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
links.why: Canonical production design for partitioning, replication, hinted handoff, vector clocks, and tunable consistency.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data
links.why: Shows sorted-table storage, tablets, metadata, and large-scale serving for structured data.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Spanner: Google's Globally Distributed Database
links.why: Reference for globally replicated transactions, external consistency, and geographically distributed state.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm
links.why: Readable consensus reference for leader election, log replication, membership changes, and safety.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Cassandra: A Decentralized Structured Storage System
links.why: Useful for high-write distributed storage, eventual consistency, and wide-column message histories.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Redis Cluster Specification
links.why: Covers hash slots, failover, and operational constraints for sharded cache-like systems.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.

links.groupDescription: Production docs showing how Dynamo-style ideas appear in real distributed KV and wide-column systems.
links.title: Dynamo
links.why: Connects consistent hashing, replica placement, best-effort convergence, anti-entropy repair, and R/W/N consistency to a production Dynamo-lineage database.

links.groupDescription: Production docs showing how Dynamo-style ideas appear in real distributed KV and wide-column systems.
links.title: ScyllaDB Ring Architecture - Overview
links.why: Shows how a Cassandra-compatible system maps data onto a shared-nothing ring and reasons about ownership when clusters scale.

links.groupDescription: Production docs showing how Dynamo-style ideas appear in real distributed KV and wide-column systems.
links.title: Gossip in ScyllaDB
links.why: Explains decentralized node-state exchange, metadata versioning, and why gossip avoids a single liveness registry.

links.groupDescription: References for version causality, sibling handling, tombstones, and multi-region convergence choices.
links.title: Conflict Resolution
links.why: Gives practical guidance for causal context, vector-clock terminology, siblings, and when applications must merge concurrent versions.

links.groupDescription: Operational mechanisms that keep replicas converging after missed writes, outages, and deletes.
links.title: Hints
links.why: Describes hinted handoff, retention windows, and how hints reduce but do not replace later anti-entropy repair.

links.groupDescription: Operational mechanisms that keep replicas converging after missed writes, outages, and deletes.
links.title: Repair
links.why: Shows how full and incremental repairs compare Merkle trees, stream divergent ranges, and protect against missed hints and tombstone expiry.

links.groupDescription: Operational mechanisms that keep replicas converging after missed writes, outages, and deletes.
links.title: Read repair
links.why: Explains foreground reconciliation on reads, monotonic quorum-read expectations, and the tradeoff between monotonicity and write atomicity.

links.groupDescription: Operational mechanisms that keep replicas converging after missed writes, outages, and deletes.
links.title: Tombstones
links.why: Grounds delete handling in a replicated system: delete markers, grace periods, repair timing, and the risk of resurrecting stale data.

links.groupDescription: Local storage-engine references for understanding latency, write amplification, compaction, and disk layout.
links.title: Compaction
links.why: Covers leveled and tiered compaction tradeoffs, write amplification, read amplification, and why compaction must be budgeted in the KV store.

links.groupDescription: Production systems and managed services that expose real partitioning, scaling, and consistency tradeoffs.
links.title: Scaling DynamoDB: How partitions, hot keys, and split for heat impact performance (Part 3)
links.why: Provides a production view of partition-key design, hot partitions, adaptive capacity, burst capacity, and split-for-heat behavior.

links.groupDescription: Production systems and managed services that expose real partitioning, scaling, and consistency tradeoffs.
links.title: Global tables - multi-active, multi-Region replication
links.why: Shows a managed multi-active replication model, asynchronous cross-region propagation, consistency modes, and last-writer-wins conflict handling.

links.groupDescription: Production systems and managed services that expose real partitioning, scaling, and consistency tradeoffs.
links.title: Open Sourcing Venice: LinkedIn's Derived Data Platform
links.why: Describes a production derived-data store with key-value access, high read volume, horizontal scale, and eventual consistency tradeoffs.

links.groupDescription: Production systems and managed services that expose real partitioning, scaling, and consistency tradeoffs.
links.title: Scaling memcache at Facebook
links.why: Useful contrast for distributed cache-like KV design, client routing, hot-key pressure, and operational scaling at very high request rates.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: SWIM: Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Process Group Membership Protocol
links.why: Foundational membership and failure-detection paper for scalable, gossip-style cluster liveness without a central coordinator.

links.groupDescription: References for keeping distributed services reliable under overload, retries, background work, and partial failure.
links.title: Timeouts, retries, and backoff with jitter
links.why: Explains timeout selection, retry amplification, backoff, and jitter, which directly affect coordinator-to-replica calls and client retry behavior.

links.groupDescription: References for keeping distributed services reliable under overload, retries, background work, and partial failure.
links.title: Monitoring
links.why: Enumerates metrics for tables, commit logs, compaction, storage, hints, and clients, grounding the operations step in concrete signals.

links.groupDescription: References for keeping distributed services reliable under overload, retries, background work, and partial failure.
links.title: Backups
links.why: Covers snapshot and incremental-backup mechanics for immutable SSTables, useful for per-range backup and restore planning.
